title: The Big Bang
subtitle: Origin of the universe and the evidence

steps.title: Quick question first…
steps.question: Which observation provides the strongest evidence for the Big Bang theory?
options: The Sun produces energy by nuclear fusion
Red-shift of galaxies and the cosmic microwave background radiation
The Moon orbits the Earth in a regular period
Stars come in many different sizes and colours
steps.misconceptionFeedback: ⚠️ Common misconception! The main evidence for the Big Bang is the red-shift of distant galaxies (showing the universe is expanding) and the cosmic microwave background radiation (CMB), which is the afterglow of heat from the early universe detected as microwave radiation in all directions.
steps.correctFeedback: ✓ Correct! Red-shift shows the universe is expanding, and the CMB is the remnant heat radiation from the early universe — both strongly support the Big Bang theory.
steps.bestSource: BEST resource: AQA Physics specification 4.8.3 — The Big Bang

steps.title: The Big Bang
steps.bodyHTML: The Big Bang theory proposes that the universe began approximately 13.8 billion years ago from an extremely hot, dense point and has been expanding ever since. Key evidence includes the red-shift of almost all galaxies (showing they are receding) and the cosmic microwave background (CMB) radiation, a uniform microwave signal detected from all directions in space, which is the cooled remnant of radiation from the early universe. The expansion of the universe continues to this day.
commonErrors: Thinking the Big Bang was an explosion in space — it was the expansion of space itself from a single point
Confusing red-shift (evidence for expansion) with CMB (evidence for early hot universe) — both are needed
Forgetting the approximate age of the universe (13.8 billion years)

aqa.questionText: Describe two pieces of evidence that support the Big Bang theory and explain how each supports the theory.

steps.label: Evidence 1: Red-shift
steps.content: Almost all distant galaxies show red-shift, meaning their light is stretched to longer wavelengths.

steps.label: How red-shift supports the Big Bang
steps.content: This shows galaxies are moving away from us in all directions, meaning the universe is expanding — consistent with it having originated from a single point.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — explains expansion from a single point

steps.label: Evidence 2: Cosmic microwave background radiation
steps.content: Microwave radiation of a specific temperature is detected uniformly from all directions in space.

steps.label: How CMB supports the Big Bang
steps.content: This is the cooled remnant radiation from the extremely hot, early universe, consistent with a hot Big Bang origin.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — CMB as cooled remnant of hot early universe

aqa.finalAnswer: Red-shift → universe is expanding (evidence of origin from single point). CMB → cooled remnant radiation from the hot early universe.

aqa.questionText: What is the cosmic microwave background radiation?

options.label: A
options.text: Radiation emitted by the Sun that has been red-shifted to microwave frequencies

options.label: B
options.text: Microwave signals from distant radio galaxies bouncing off the edge of the universe

options.label: C
options.text: Uniform microwave radiation from all directions, the remnant heat of the early universe

options.label: D
options.text: Radiation produced when dark matter annihilates in deep space

markSchemeSteps: CMB is the remnant heat radiation from the hot early universe, now cooled to microwave frequencies and detected uniformly in all directions ✓
aqa.commonMistake: Option A confuses CMB with solar radiation — the CMB exists throughout all of space and predates any stars.
